name: Bug: verify.py fails with 'source: not found' on dash systems

task_definition.title: Bug: verify.py fails with 'source: not found' on dash systems
task_definition.description: The verify step fails with 'source: not found' on Ubuntu systems where /bin/sh is dash. Fix create_subprocess_shell in verify.py to use executable='/bin/bash' so shell builtins like 'source' work correctly.

verify_commands.test: cd orchestrator && uv run pytest tests/ -x
verify_commands.lint: cd orchestrator && uv run ruff check src/
verify_commands.typecheck: cd orchestrator && uv run pyright src/

plan.title: Bug: verify.py fails with 'source: not found' on dash systems
plan.analysis: The bug is in orchestrator/src/orchestrator/verify.py, line 36. The `_run_cmd()` function calls `asyncio.create_subprocess_shell()` without specifying the `executable` parameter. On Ubuntu/Debian systems where /bin/sh is symlinked to dash (not bash), shell builtins like `source` fail with 'source: not found' because dash only supports the POSIX `.` command. The fix is to add `executable='/bin/bash'` to the `create_subprocess_shell()` call. There are currently NO unit tests for verify.py — it is only mocked in the e2e workflow tests (test_workflow_e2e.py). We need to create a new test file `orchestrator/tests/test_verify.py` with tests that verify: (1) the bash executable is passed to subprocess creation, and (2) bash-specific builtins like `source` work when executed through `_run_cmd`. The fix is a one-line change but the test coverage gap should be addressed.

steps.description: Write failing test in orchestrator/tests/test_verify.py that patches asyncio.create_subprocess_shell and asserts it is called with executable='/bin/bash'. Also write an integration test that runs a command using `source` (or bash-only syntax) through _run_cmd and asserts it succeeds (returncode 0). Both tests should fail currently since _run_cmd does not pass executable='/bin/bash'.

Fix _run_cmd() in orchestrator/src/orchestrator/verify.py by adding executable='/bin/bash' to the asyncio.create_subprocess_shell() call on line 36. This ensures bash is used as the shell interpreter regardless of what /bin/sh points to, making `source` and other bash builtins work on dash systems.

Write additional tests for _run_cmd edge cases: (1) test that timeout handling still works correctly with the bash executable, (2) test that run_verification passes results through correctly. Verify all tests in orchestrator/tests/test_verify.py pass.

design_decisions.decision: Use executable='/bin/bash' rather than rewriting commands to be POSIX-compatible
design_decisions.rationale: The verification commands (pytest, ruff, pyright) may use shell features that depend on bash. Forcing bash is simpler and more robust than auditing all possible user-configured commands for POSIX compliance. The task description explicitly requests this approach.

design_decisions.decision: Create a new test_verify.py rather than adding to test_workflow_e2e.py
design_decisions.rationale: verify.py has zero unit test coverage currently — only mocked in e2e tests. A dedicated test file follows the existing pattern (test_config.py, test_git_ops.py, test_artifacts.py) and enables focused testing of _run_cmd and run_verification.

design_decisions.decision: Test the executable parameter via mock rather than only integration tests
design_decisions.rationale: An integration test that relies on the system having dash as /bin/sh would not be portable. Mocking create_subprocess_shell lets us assert the executable parameter is passed correctly on any CI system. We supplement with an integration test using bash-specific syntax (e.g., source /dev/null) that would fail under dash but pass under bash.

reuse.how: Reuse the VerifyResult construction pattern and pytest-asyncio test class style
Create a minimal OrchestratorConfig for run_verification tests
Test _run_cmd directly since it is a module-level async function (not private to a class)
